| session_id=session_id,]{.c25}                                         |
|                                                                       |
| [       session=session, session_service=session_service]{.c25}       |
|                                                                       |
| [   )]{.c25}                                                          |
|                                                                       |
| [)]{.c25}                                                             |
|                                                                       |
| []{.c25}                                                              |
|                                                                       |
| [\# 3. Execute the tool]{.c25}                                        |
|                                                                       |
| [log_user_login(mock_context)]{.c25}                                  |
|                                                                       |
| []{.c25}                                                              |
|                                                                       |
| [\# 4. Check the updated state]{.c25}                                 |
|                                                                       |
| [updated_session = session_service.get_session(app_name, user_id,     |
| session_id)]{.c25}                                                    |
|                                                                       |
| [print(f\"State after tool execution:                                 |
| {updated_session.state}\")]{.c25}                                     |
|                                                                       |
| []{.c25}                                                              |
|                                                                       |
| [\# Expected output will show the same state change as the ]{.c25}    |
|                                                                       |
| [\# \"Before\" case,]{.c25}                                           |
|                                                                       |
| [\# but the code organization is significantly cleaner ]{.c25}        |
|                                                                       |
| [\# and more robust.]{.c25}                                           |
+-----------------------------------------------------------------------+

[]{.c8}

[This code demonstrates a tool-based approach for managing user session
state in an application. It defines a function ]{.c12}
[log_user_login]{.c42} [,]{.c12} [ which acts as a tool. This tool is
responsible for updating the session state when a user logs in.]{.c8}

[The function takes a ]{.c12} [ToolContext]{.c12} [ object, provided by
the ADK, to access and modify the session\'s state dictionary. Inside
the tool, it increments a ]{.c12} [user:login_count]{.c42} [, sets the
]{.c12} [t]{.c12} [ask_status]{.c42} [ to \"active\", records the
]{.c12} [user:last_login_ts]{.c42} [ (timestamp)]{.c42} [, and adds a
temporary flag ]{.c12} [temp:validation_needed]{.c12} [. ]{.c8}

[]{.c8}

[The demonstration part of the code simulates how this tool would be
used. It sets up an in-memory session service and creates an initial
session with some predefined state. A ]{.c12} [ToolContext]{.c12} [ is
then manually created to mimic the environment in which the ADK Runner
would execute the tool. The ]{.c12} [log_user_login]{.c12} [ function is
called with this mock context. Finally, the code retrieves the session
again to show that the state has been updated by the tool\'s execution.
The goal is to show how encapsulating state changes within tools makes
the code cleaner and more organized compared to directly manipulating
state outside of tools]{.c12} [.]{.c43}

[]{.c43}

[Note that d]{.c12} [irect modification of the \`session.state\`
dictionary after retrieving a session is strongly discouraged as it
bypasses the standard event processing mechanism.]{.c12} [ ]{.c12} [Such
direct changes will not be recorded in the session\'s event history, may
not be persisted by the selected \`SessionService\`, could lead to
concurrency issues, and will not update essential metadata such as
timestamps.]{.c12} [ ]{.c12} [The recommended methods for updating the
session state are using the \`output_key\` parameter on an \`LlmAgent\`
(specifically for the agent\'s final text responses) or including state
changes within \`EventActions.state_delta\` when appending an event via
\`session_service.append_event()\`.]{.c12} [ ]{.c12} [The
\`session.state\` should primarily be used for reading existing
data.]{.c12}

[]{.c7}

[To recap, when designing your state, keep it simple, use basic data
types, give your keys clear names and use prefixes correctly, avoid deep
nesting, and always update state using the append_event process.]{.c15}

## [Memory: Long-Term Knowledge with MemoryService]{.c10} {#Chapter8MemoryManagement.xhtml_h.rrlwum92j6p3 .c30}

[In agent systems, the Session component maintains a record of the
current chat history (events) and temporary data (state) specific to a
single conversation.]{.c4} [ ]{.c4} [However, for agents to retain
information across multiple interactions or access external data,
long-term knowledge management is necessary.]{.c4} [ ]{.c4} [This is
facilitated by the MemoryService.]{.c4}

[]{.c15}

+-----------------------------------------------------------------------+
| [\# Example: Using InMemoryMemoryService]{.c25}                       |
|                                                                       |
| [\# This is suitable for local development and testing where data     |
| ]{.c25}                                                               |
|                                                                       |
| [\# persistence across application restarts is not required. ]{.c25}  |
|                                                                       |
| [\# Memory content is lost when the app stops.]{.c25}                 |
|                                                                       |
| [from google.adk.memory import InMemoryMemoryService]{.c25}           |
|                                                                       |
| [memory_service = InMemoryMemoryService()]{.c25}                      |
+-----------------------------------------------------------------------+

[]{.c15}

[Session and State can be conceptualized as short-term memory for a
single chat session, whereas the Long-Term Knowledge managed by the
MemoryService functions as a persistent and searchable repository.]{.c4}
[ ]{.c4} [This repository may contain information from multiple past
interactions or external sources.]{.c4} [ ]{.c4} [The MemoryService, as
defined by the BaseMemoryService interface, establishes a standard for
managing this searchable, long-term knowledge.]{.c4} [ ]{.c4} [Its
primary functions include adding information, which involves extracting
content from a session and storing it using the add_session_to_memory
method, and retrieving information, which allows an agent to query the
store and receive relevant data using the search_memory method.]{.c4}

[]{.c15}

[The ADK offers several implementations for creating this long-term
knowledge store.]{.c4} [ ]{.c4} [The InMemoryMemoryService provides a
temporary storage solution suitable for testing purposes, but data is
not preserved across application restarts.]{.c4} [ ]{.c4} [For
production environments, the VertexAiRagMemoryService is typically
utilized.]{.c4} [ ]{.c4} [This service leverages Google Cloud\'s
Retrieval Augmented Generation (RAG) service, enabling scalable,
persistent, and semantic search capabilities]{.c4} [ (]{.c4} [Also,
refer to the chapter 14 on RAG]{.c15} [).]{.c15}

+----------------------------------------------------------------------------------+
| [\# Example: Using VertexAiRagMemoryService]{.c25}                               |
|                                                                                  |
| [\# This is suitable for scalable production on GCP, leveraging]{.c25}           |
|                                                                                  |
| [\# Vertex AI RAG (Retrieval Augmented Generation) for persistent, ]{.c25}       |
|                                                                                  |
| [\# searchable memory.]{.c25}                                                    |
|                                                                                  |
| [\# Requires: pip install google-adk\[vertexai\], GCP ]{.c25}                    |
|                                                                                  |
| [\# setup/authentication, and a Vertex AI RAG Corpus.]{.c25}                     |
|                                                                                  |
| [from google.adk.memory import VertexAiRagMemoryService]{.c25}                   |
|                                                                                  |
| []{.c25}                                                                         |
|                                                                                  |
| [\# The resource name of your Vertex AI RAG Corpus]{.c25}                        |
|                                                                                  |
| [RAG_CORPUS_RESOURCE_NAME =                                                      |
| \"projects/your-gcp-project-id/locations/us-central1/ragCorpora/your-corpus-id\" |
| \# Replace with your Corpus resource name]{.c25}                                 |
|                                                                                  |
| []{.c25}                                                                         |
|                                                                                  |
| [\# Optional configuration for retrieval behavior]{.c25}                         |
|                                                                                  |
| [SIMILARITY_TOP_K = 5 \# Number of top results to retrieve]{.c25}                |
|                                                                                  |
| [VECTOR_DISTANCE_THRESHOLD = 0.7 \# Threshold for vector similarity]{.c25}       |
|                                                                                  |
| []{.c25}                                                                         |
|                                                                                  |
| [memory_service = VertexAiRagMemoryService(]{.c25}                               |
|                                                                                  |
| [   rag_corpus=RAG_CORPUS_RESOURCE_NAME,]{.c25}                                  |
|                                                                                  |
| [   similarity_top_k=SIMILARITY_TOP_K,]{.c25}                                    |
|                                                                                  |
| [   vector_distance_threshold=VECTOR_DISTANCE_THRESHOLD]{.c25}                   |
|                                                                                  |
| [)]{.c25}                                                                        |
|                                                                                  |
| [\# When using this service, methods like add_session_to_memory ]{.c25}          |
|                                                                                  |
| [\# and search_memory will interact with the specified Vertex AI ]{.c25}         |
|                                                                                  |
| [\# RAG Corpus.]{.c25}                                                           |
+----------------------------------------------------------------------------------+

[]{.c2}

# [Hands-on code: Memory Management in LangChain and LangGraph]{.c10} {#Chapter8MemoryManagement.xhtml_h.jv3rycbe9ib6 .c44}

[In LangChain and LangGraph, Memory is a critical component for creating
intelligent and natural-feeling conversational applications. It allows
an AI agent to remember information from past interactions, learn from
feedback, and adapt to user preferences. LangChain\'s memory feature
provides the foundation for this by referencing a stored history to
enrich current prompts and then recording the latest exchange for future
use. As agents handle more complex tasks, this capability becomes
essential for both efficiency and user satisfaction.]{.c15}

[]{.c15}

[Short-Term Memory:]{.c46} [ This is thread-scoped, meaning it tracks
the ongoing conversation within a single session or thread. It provides
immediate context, but a full history can challenge an LLM\'s context
window, potentially leading to errors or poor performance. LangGraph
manages short-term memory as part of the agent\'s state, which is
persisted via a checkpointer, allowing a thread to be resumed at any
time.]{.c15}

[]{.c15}

[Long-Term Memory:]{.c46} [ This stores user-specific or
application-level data across sessions and is shared between
conversational threads. It is saved in custom \"namespaces\" and can be
recalled at any time in any thread. LangGraph provides stores to save
and recall long-term memories, enabling agents to retain knowledge
indefinitely.]{.c15}

[]{.c15}

[LangChain provides several tools for managing conversation history,
ranging from manual control to automated integration within
chains.]{.c15}

[]{.c15}

[ChatMessageHistory: Manual Memory Management. ]{.c46} [For direct and
simple control over a conversation\'s history outside of a formal chain,
the ChatMessageHistory class is ideal. It allows for the manual tracking
of dialogue exchanges.]{.c15}

[]{.c15}

+-----------------------------------------------------------------------+
| [from langchain.memory import ChatMessageHistory]{.c25}               |
|                                                                       |
| []{.c25}                                                              |
|                                                                       |
| [\# Initialize the history object]{.c25}                              |
|                                                                       |
| [history = ChatMessageHistory()]{.c25}                                |
|                                                                       |
| []{.c25}                                                              |
|                                                                       |
| [\# Add user and AI messages]{.c25}                                   |
|                                                                       |
| [history.add_user_message(\"I\'m heading to New York next             |
| week.\")]{.c25}                                                       |
|                                                                       |
| [history.add_ai_message(\"Great! It\'s a fantastic city.\")]{.c25}    |
|                                                                       |
| []{.c25}                                                              |
|                                                                       |
| [\# Access the list of messages]{.c25}                                |
|                                                                       |
| [print(history.messages)]{.c25}                                       |
+-----------------------------------------------------------------------+

[]{.c2}

[ConversationBufferMemory: Automated Memory for Chains]{.c46} [. ]{.c4}
[For integrating memory directly into chains, ConversationBufferMemory
is a common choice. It holds a buffer of the conversation and makes it
available to your prompt. Its behavior can be customized with two key
parameters:]{.c15}

- [memory_key]{.c4} [: A string that specifies the variable name in your
  prompt that will hold the chat history. It defaults to
  \"history\".]{.c15}
- [return_messages]{.c4} [: A boolean that dictates the format of the
  history.]{.c15}

<!-- -->

- [If False (the default), it returns a single formatted string, which
  is ideal for standard LLMs.]{.c15}
- [If True, it returns a list of message objects, which is the
  recommended format for Chat Models.]{.c15}

[]{.c15}

+-----------------------------------------------------------------------+
| [from langchain.memory import ConversationBufferMemory]{.c25}         |
|                                                                       |
| []{.c25}                                                              |
|                                                                       |
| [\# Initialize memory]{.c25}                                          |
|                                                                       |
| [memory = ConversationBufferMemory()]{.c25}                           |
|                                                                       |
| []{.c25}                                                              |
|                                                                       |
| [\# Save a conversation turn]{.c25}                                   |
|                                                                       |
| [memory.save_context({\"input\": \"What\'s the weather like?\"},      |
| {\"output\": \"It\'s sunny today.\"})]{.c25}                          |
|                                                                       |
| []{.c25}                                                              |
|                                                                       |
| [\# Load the memory as a string]{.c25}                                |
|                                                                       |
| [print(memory.load_memory_variables({}))]{.c25}                       |
+-----------------------------------------------------------------------+

[]{.c15}

[]{.c2}

[Integrating this memory into an LLMChain allows the model to access the
conversation\'s history and provide contextually relevant
responses]{.c15}

[]{.c15}

+-----------------------------------------------------------------------+
| [from langchain_openai import OpenAI]{.c25}                           |
|                                                                       |
| [from langchain.chains import LLMChain]{.c25}                         |
|                                                                       |
| [from langchain.prompts import PromptTemplate]{.c25}                  |
|                                                                       |
| [from langchain.memory import ConversationBufferMemory]{.c25}         |
|                                                                       |
| []{.c25}                                                              |
|                                                                       |
| [\# 1. Define LLM and Prompt]{.c25}                                   |
|                                                                       |
| [llm = OpenAI(temperature=0)]{.c25}                                   |
|                                                                       |
| [template = \"\"\"You are a helpful travel agent.]{.c25}              |
|                                                                       |
| []{.c25}                                                              |
|                                                                       |
| [Previous conversation:]{.c25}                                        |
|                                                                       |
| [{history}]{.c25}                                                     |
|                                                                       |
| []{.c25}                                                              |
|                                                                       |
| [New question: {question}]{.c25}                                      |
|                                                                       |
| [Response:\"\"\"]{.c25}                                               |
|                                                                       |
| [prompt = PromptTemplate.from_template(template)]{.c25}               |
|                                                                       |
| []{.c25}                                                              |
|                                                                       |
| [\# 2. Configure Memory]{.c25}                                        |
|                                                                       |
| [\# The memory_key \"history\" matches the variable in the            |
| prompt]{.c25}                                                         |
|                                                                       |
| [memory = ConversationBufferMemory(memory_key=\"history\")]{.c25}     |
|                                                                       |
| []{.c25}                                                              |
|                                                                       |
| [\# 3. Build the Chain]{.c25}                                         |
|                                                                       |
| [conversation = LLMChain(llm=llm, prompt=prompt,                      |
| memory=memory)]{.c25}                                                 |
|                                                                       |
| []{.c25}                                                              |
|                                                                       |
| [\# 4. Run the Conversation]{.c25}                                    |
|                                                                       |
| [response = conversation.predict(question=\"I want to book a          |
| flight.\")]{.c25}                                                     |
|                                                                       |
| [print(response)]{.c25}                                               |
|                                                                       |
| [response = conversation.predict(question=\"My name is Sam, by the    |
| way.\")]{.c25}                                                        |
|                                                                       |
| [print(response)]{.c25}                                               |
|                                                                       |
| [response = conversation.predict(question=\"What was my name          |
| again?\")]{.c25}                                                      |
|                                                                       |
| [print(response)]{.c25}                                               |
+-----------------------------------------------------------------------+

[]{.c2}

[For improved effectiveness with chat models, it is recommended to use a
structured list of message objects by setting
\`return_messages=True\`.]{.c4}

[]{.c2}

+-----------------------------------------------------------------------+
| [from langchain_openai import ChatOpenAI]{.c39}                       |
|                                                                       |
| [from langchain.chains import LLMChain]{.c39}                         |
|                                                                       |
| [from langchain.memory import ConversationBufferMemory]{.c39}         |
|                                                                       |
| [from langchain_core.prompts import (]{.c39}                          |
|                                                                       |
| [   ChatPromptTemplate,]{.c39}                                        |
|                                                                       |
| [   MessagesPlaceholder,]{.c39}                                       |
|                                                                       |
| [   SystemMessagePromptTemplate,]{.c39}                               |
|                                                                       |
| [   HumanMessagePromptTemplate,]{.c39}                                |
|                                                                       |
| [)]{.c39}                                                             |
|                                                                       |
| []{.c39}                                                              |
|                                                                       |
| [\# 1. Define Chat Model and Prompt]{.c39}                            |
|                                                                       |
| [llm = ChatOpenAI()]{.c39}                                            |
|                                                                       |
| [prompt = ChatPromptTemplate(]{.c39}                                  |
|                                                                       |
| [   messages=\[]{.c39}                                                |
|                                                                       |
| [       SystemMessagePromptTemplate.from_template(\"You are a         |
| friendly assistant.\"),]{.c39}                                        |
|                                                                       |
| [       MessagesPlaceholder(variable_name=\"chat_history\"),]{.c39}   |
|                                                                       |
| [                                                                     |
|  HumanMessagePromptTemplate.from_template(\"{question}\")]{.c39}      |
|                                                                       |
| [   \]]{.c39}                                                         |
|                                                                       |
| [)]{.c39}                                                             |
|                                                                       |
| []{.c39}                                                              |
|                                                                       |
| [\# 2. Configure Memory]{.c39}                                        |
|                                                                       |
| [\# return_messages=True is essential for chat models]{.c39}          |
|                                                                       |
| [memory = ConversationBufferMemory(memory_key=\"chat_history\",       |
| return_messages=True)]{.c39}                                          |
|                                                                       |
| []{.c39}                                                              |
|                                                                       |
| [\# 3. Build the Chain]{.c39}                                         |
|                                                                       |
| [conversation = LLMChain(llm=llm, prompt=prompt,                      |
| memory=memory)]{.c39}                                                 |
|                                                                       |
| []{.c39}                                                              |
|                                                                       |
| [\# 4. Run the Conversation]{.c39}                                    |
|                                                                       |
| [response = conversation.predict(question=\"Hi, I\'m Jane.\")]{.c39}  |
|                                                                       |
| [print(response)]{.c39}                                               |
|                                                                       |
| [response = conversation.predict(question=\"Do you remember my        |
| name?\")]{.c39}                                                       |
|                                                                       |
| [print(response)]{.c39}                                               |
+-----------------------------------------------------------------------+

[]{.c2}

[Types of Long-Term Memory]{.c46} [: ]{.c4} [Long-term memory allows
systems to retain information across different conversations, providing
a deeper level of context and personalization. It can be broken down
into three types analogous to human memory:]{.c15}

[]{.c15}

- [Semantic Memory: Remembering Facts:]{.c46} [ ]{.c4} [This involves
  retaining specific facts and concepts, such as user preferences or
  domain knowledge. It is used to ground an agent\'s responses, leading
  to more personalized and relevant interactions. This information can
  be managed as a continuously updated user \"profile\" (a JSON
  document) or as a \"collection\" of individual factual
  documents.]{.c15}
- [Episodic Memory: Remembering Experiences: ]{.c46} [This involves
  recalling past events or actions. For AI agents, episodic memory is
  often used to remember how to accomplish a task. In practice, it\'s
  frequently implemented through ]{.c4} [few-shot example
  prompting]{.c4} [, where an agent learns from past successful
  interaction sequences to perform tasks correctly.]{.c15}
- [Procedural Memory: Remembering Rules:  ]{.c46} [This is the memory of
  how to perform tasks---the agent\'s core instructions and behaviors,
  often contained in its system prompt. It\'s common for agents to
  modify their own prompts to adapt and improve. An effective technique
  is ]{.c4} [\"Reflection,\"]{.c4} [ where an agent is prompted with its
  current instructions and recent interactions, then asked to refine its
  own instructions.]{.c15}

[]{.c15}

[Below is pseudo-code demonstrating how an agent might use reflection to
update its procedural memory stored in a LangGraph BaseStore]{.c15}

[]{.c15}

+------------------------------------------------------------------------------------+
| [\# Node that updates the agent\'s instructions]{.c25}                             |
|                                                                                    |
| [def update_instructions(state: State, store: BaseStore):]{.c25}                   |
|                                                                                    |
| [   namespace = (\"instructions\",)]{.c25}                                         |
|                                                                                    |
| [   # Get the current instructions from the store]{.c25}                           |
|                                                                                    |
| [   current_instructions = store.search(namespace)\[0\]]{.c25}                     |
|                                                                                    |
| [  ]{.c25}                                                                         |
|                                                                                    |
| [   # Create a prompt to ask the LLM to reflect on the conversation]{.c25}         |
|                                                                                    |
| [   # and generate new, improved instructions]{.c25}                               |
|                                                                                    |
| [   prompt = prompt_template.format(]{.c25}                                        |
|                                                                                    |
| [       instructions=current_instructions.value\[\"instructions\"\],]{.c25}        |
|                                                                                    |
| [       conversation=state\[\"messages\"\]]{.c25}                                  |
|                                                                                    |
| [   )]{.c25}                                                                       |
|                                                                                    |
| [  ]{.c25}                                                                         |
|                                                                                    |
| [   # Get the new instructions from the LLM]{.c25}                                 |
|                                                                                    |
| [   output = llm.invoke(prompt)]{.c25}                                             |
|                                                                                    |
| [   new_instructions = output\[\'new_instructions\'\]]{.c25}                       |
|                                                                                    |
| [  ]{.c25}                                                                         |
|                                                                                    |
| [   # Save the updated instructions back to the store]{.c25}                       |
|                                                                                    |
| [   store.put((\"agent_instructions\",), \"agent_a\", {\"instructions\":           |
| new_instructions})]{.c25}                                                          |
|                                                                                    |
| []{.c25}                                                                           |
|                                                                                    |
| [\# Node that uses the instructions to generate a response]{.c25}                  |
|                                                                                    |
| [def call_model(state: State, store: BaseStore):]{.c25}                            |
|                                                                                    |
| [   namespace = (\"agent_instructions\", )]{.c25}                                  |
|                                                                                    |
| [   # Retrieve the latest instructions from the store]{.c25}                       |
|                                                                                    |
| [   instructions = store.get(namespace, key=\"agent_a\")\[0\]]{.c25}               |
|                                                                                    |
| [  ]{.c25}                                                                         |
|                                                                                    |
| [   # Use the retrieved instructions to format the prompt]{.c25}                   |
|                                                                                    |
| [   prompt =                                                                       |
| prompt_template.format(instructions=instructions.value\[\"instructions\"\])]{.c25} |
|                                                                                    |
| [   # \... application logic continues]{.c25}                                      |
+------------------------------------------------------------------------------------+

[]{.c15}

[]{.c2}

[]{.c2}

[LangGraph stores long-term memories as JSON documents in a store. Each
memory is organized under a custom namespace (like a folder) and a
distinct key (like a filename). This hierarchical structure allows for
easy organization and retrieval of information. The following code
demonstrates how to use InMemoryStore to put, get, and search for
memories.]{.c15}

+-----------------------------------------------------------------------+
| [from langgraph.store.memory import InMemoryStore]{.c25}              |
|                                                                       |
| []{.c25}                                                              |
|                                                                       |
| [\# A placeholder for a real embedding function]{.c25}                |
|                                                                       |
| [def embed(texts: list\[str\]) -\> list\[list\[float\]\]:]{.c25}      |
|                                                                       |
| [   # In a real application, use a proper embedding model]{.c25}      |
|                                                                       |
| [   return \[\[1.0, 2.0\] for \_ in texts\]]{.c25}                    |
|                                                                       |
| []{.c25}                                                              |
|                                                                       |
| [\# Initialize an in-memory store. For production, use a              |
| database-backed store.]{.c25}                                         |
|                                                                       |
| [store = InMemoryStore(index={\"embed\": embed, \"dims\": 2})]{.c25}  |
|                                                                       |
| []{.c25}                                                              |
|                                                                       |
| [\# Define a namespace for a specific user and application            |
| context]{.c25}                                                        |
|                                                                       |
| [user_id = \"my-user\"]{.c25}                                         |
|                                                                       |
| [application_context = \"chitchat\"]{.c25}                            |
|                                                                       |
| [namespace = (user_id, application_context)]{.c25}                    |
|                                                                       |
| []{.c25}                                                              |
|                                                                       |
| [\# 1. Put a memory into the store]{.c25}                             |
|                                                                       |
| [store.put(]{.c25}                                                    |
|                                                                       |
| [   namespace,]{.c25}                                                 |
|                                                                       |
| [   \"a-memory\",  # The key for this memory]{.c25}                   |
|                                                                       |
| [   {]{.c25}                                                          |
|                                                                       |
| [       \"rules\": \[]{.c25}                                          |
|                                                                       |
| [           \"User likes short, direct language\",]{.c25}             |
|                                                                       |
| [           \"User only speaks English & python\",]{.c25}             |
|                                                                       |
| [       \],]{.c25}                                                    |
|                                                                       |
| [       \"my-key\": \"my-value\",]{.c25}                              |
|                                                                       |
| [   },]{.c25}                                                         |
|                                                                       |
| [)]{.c25}                                                             |
|                                                                       |
| []{.c25}                                                              |
|                                                                       |
| [\# 2. Get the memory by its namespace and key]{.c25}                 |
|                                                                       |
| [item = store.get(namespace, \"a-memory\")]{.c25}                     |
|                                                                       |
| [print(\"Retrieved Item:\", item)]{.c25}                              |
|                                                                       |
| []{.c25}                                                              |
|                                                                       |
| [\# 3. Search for memories within the namespace, filtering by         |
| content]{.c25}                                                        |
|                                                                       |
| [\# and sorting by vector similarity to the query.]{.c25}             |
|                                                                       |
| [items = store.search(]{.c25}                                         |
|                                                                       |
| [   namespace,]{.c25}                                                 |
|                                                                       |
| [   filter={\"my-key\": \"my-value\"},]{.c25}                         |
|                                                                       |
| [   query=\"language preferences\"]{.c25}                             |
|                                                                       |
| [)]{.c25}                                                             |
|                                                                       |
| [print(\"Search Results:\", items)]{.c25}                             |
+-----------------------------------------------------------------------+

[]{.c15}

# [Vertex Memory Bank]{.c10} {#Chapter8MemoryManagement.xhtml_h.4gespwoz8hs3 .c44}

[Memory Bank, a managed service in the Vertex AI Agent Engine, provides
agents with persistent, long-term memory. The service uses Gemini models
to asynchronously analyze conversation histories to extract key facts
and user preferences.]{.c15}

[This information is stored persistently, organized by a defined scope
like user ID, and intelligently updated to consolidate new data and
resolve contradictions. Upon starting a new session, the agent retrieves
relevant memories through either a full data recall or a similarity
search using embeddings. This process allows an agent to maintain
continuity across sessions and personalize responses based on recalled
information. ]{.c15}

[The agent\'s runner interacts with the VertexAiMemoryBankService, which
is initialized first.]{.c15} [ ]{.c15} [This service handles the
automatic storage of memories generated during the agent\'s
conversations.]{.c15} [ ]{.c15} [Each memory is tagged with a unique
USER_ID and APP_NAME, ensuring accurate retrieval in the future.]{.c15}

+-----------------------------------------------------------------------+
| [from google.adk.memory import VertexAiMemoryBankService]{.c25}       |
|                                                                       |
| []{.c25}                                                              |
|                                                                       |
| [agent_engine_id =                                                    |
| agent_engine.api_resource.name.split(\"/\")\[-1\]]{.c25}              |
|                                                                       |
| []{.c25}                                                              |
|                                                                       |
| [memory_service = VertexAiMemoryBankService(]{.c25}                   |
|                                                                       |
| [   project=\"PROJECT_ID\",]{.c25}                                    |
|                                                                       |
| [   location=\"LOCATION\",]{.c25}                                     |
|                                                                       |
| [   agent_engine_id=agent_engine_id]{.c25}                            |
|                                                                       |
| [)]{.c25}                                                             |
|                                                                       |
| []{.c25}                                                              |
|                                                                       |
| [session = await session_service.get_session(]{.c25}                  |
|                                                                       |
| [   app_name=app_name,]{.c25}                                         |
|                                                                       |
| [   user_id=\"USER_ID\",]{.c25}                                       |
|                                                                       |
| [   session_id=session.id]{.c25}                                      |
|                                                                       |
| [)]{.c25}                                                             |
|                                                                       |
| [await memory_service.add_session_to_memory(session)]{.c25}           |
+-----------------------------------------------------------------------+

[]{.c15}

[]{.c50}

[Memory Bank offers seamless integration with the Google ADK, providing
an immediate out-of-the-box experience.]{.c4} [ ]{.c4} [For users of
other agent frameworks, such as LangGraph and CrewAI, Memory Bank also
offers support through direct API calls.]{.c4} [ ]{.c4} [Online code
examples demonstrating these integrations are readily available for
interested readers.]{.c4}

# [At a Glance]{.c10} {#Chapter8MemoryManagement.xhtml_h.b8ybou4j5y5y .c44}

[What]{.c46} [: ]{.c4} [Agentic systems need to remember information
from past interactions to perform complex tasks and provide coherent
experiences. Without a memory mechanism, agents are stateless, unable to
maintain conversational context, learn from experience, or personalize
responses for users. This fundamentally limits them to simple, one-shot
interactions, failing to handle multi-step processes or evolving user
needs. The core problem is how to effectively manage both the immediate,
temporary information of a single conversation and the vast, persistent
knowledge gathered over time.]{.c15}

[Why: ]{.c46} [The standardized solution is to implement a
dual-component memory system that distinguishes between short-term and
long-term storage. Short-term, contextual memory holds recent
interaction data within the LLM\'s context window to maintain
conversational flow. For information that must persist, long-term memory
solutions use external databases, often vector stores, for efficient,
semantic retrieval. Agentic frameworks like the Google ADK provide
specific components to manage this, such as Session for the conversation
thread and State for its temporary data. A dedicated MemoryService is
used to interface with the long-term knowledge base, allowing the agent
to retrieve and incorporate relevant past information into its current
context.]{.c15}

[Rule of thumb: ]{.c46} [Use this pattern when an agent needs to do more
than answer a single question. It is essential for agents that must
maintain context throughout a conversation, track progress in multi-step
tasks, or personalize interactions by recalling user preferences and
history. Implement memory management whenever the agent is expected to
learn or adapt based on past successes, failures, or newly acquired
information.]{.c15}

[Visual summary]{.c52}

[ ![](14/GoogleDoc/images/image1.png){.calibre2} ]{.calibre1}

[Fig.1: Memory management design pattern]{.c15}

[]{.c2}

# [Key Takeaways]{.c10} {#Chapter8MemoryManagement.xhtml_h.t1ni145dkord .c44}

[To quickly recap the main points about memory management:]{.c15}

- [Memory is super important for agents to keep track of things, learn,
  and personalize interactions.]{.c15}
- [Conversational AI relies on both short-term memory for immediate
  context within a single chat and long-term memory for persistent
  knowledge across multiple sessions.]{.c15}
- [Short-term memory (the immediate stuff) is temporary, often limited
  by the LLM\'s context window or how the framework passes
  context.]{.c15}
- [Long-term memory (the stuff that sticks around) saves info across
  different chats using outside storage like vector databases and is
  accessed by searching.]{.c15}
- [Frameworks like ADK have specific parts like Session (the chat
  thread), State (temporary chat data), and MemoryService (the
  searchable long-term knowledge) to manage memory.]{.c15}
- [ADK\'s SessionService handles the whole life of a chat session,
  including its history (events) and temporary data (state).]{.c15}
- [ADK\'s session.state is a dictionary for temporary chat data.
  Prefixes (user:, app:, temp:) tell you where the data belongs and if
  it sticks around.]{.c15}
- [In ADK, you should update state by using EventActions.state_delta or
  output_key when adding events, not by changing the state dictionary
  directly.]{.c15}
- [ADK\'s MemoryService is for putting info into long-term storage and
  letting agents search it, often using tools.]{.c37}
- [LangChain offers practical tools like ConversationBufferMemory to
  automatically inject the history of a single conversation into a
  prompt, enabling an agent to recall immediate context.]{.c15}
- [LangGraph enables advanced, long-term memory by using a store to save
  and retrieve semantic facts, episodic experiences, or even updatable
  procedural rules across different user sessions.]{.c15}
- [Memory Bank is a managed service that provides agents with
  persistent, long-term memory by automatically extracting, storing, and
  recalling user-specific information to enable personalized, continuous
  conversations across frameworks like Google\'s ADK, LangGraph, and
  CrewAI.]{.c15}

[]{.c15}

# [Conclusion]{.c10} {#Chapter8MemoryManagement.xhtml_h.9cld0kjnbjg6 .c44}

[This chapter]{.c4} [ dove into the really important job of memory
management for agent systems, showing the difference between the
short-lived context and the knowledge that sticks around for a long
time. We talked about how these types of memory are set up and where you
see them used in building smarter agents that can remember things. We
took a detailed look at how Google ADK gives you specific pieces like
Session, State, and MemoryService to handle this.]{.c37} [ Now that
we\'ve covered how agents can remember things, both short-term and
long-term, we can move on to how they can learn and adapt. The next
pattern \"Learning and Adaptation\" is about an agent changing how it
thinks, acts, or what it knows, all based on new experiences or data.
]{.c15}

    [https://google.github.io/adk-docs/sessions/memory/](https://www.google.com/url?q=https://google.github.io/adk-docs/sessions/memory/&sa=D&source=editors&ust=1759228434230215&usg=AOvVaw25Iups_D-m3fRnXyWn7iBB){.c60}
    ]{.c59} [ ]{.c15}
2.  [LangGraph Memory, ]{.c4} [
    [https://langchain-ai.github.io/langgraph/concepts/memory/](https://www.google.com/url?q=https://langchain-ai.github.io/langgraph/concepts/memory/&sa=D&source=editors&ust=1759228434230453&usg=AOvVaw0uMqJBmSwvLUfzCTay4mhH){.c60}
    ]{.c59} [ ]{.c15}
3.  [Vertex AI Agent Engine Memory Bank, ]{.c4} [
    [https://cloud.google.com/blog/products/ai-machine-learning/vertex-ai-memory-bank-in-public-preview](https://www.google.com/url?q=https://cloud.google.com/blog/products/ai-machine-learning/vertex-ai-memory-bank-in-public-preview&sa=D&source=editors&ust=1759228434230715&usg=AOvVaw1Y-c9CB1TYOGaBkTHALCFG){.c60}
    ]{.c59} [ ]{.c15}

[]{.c15}

[]{.c15}

[]{#Chapter9LearningandAdaptation.xhtml}

[Chapter]{.calibre} [ 9: Learning and Adaptation]{.c2}

[Learning and adaptation are pivotal for enhancing the capabilities of
artificial intelligence agents.]{.c4} [ ]{.c4} [These processes enable
agents to evolve beyond predefined parameters, allowing them to improve
autonomously through experience and environmental interaction.]{.c4}
[ ]{.c4} [By learning and adapting, agents can effectively manage novel
situations and optimize their performance without constant manual
intervention.]{.c4} [ ]{.c4} [This chapter explores the principles and
mechanisms underpinning agent learning and adaptation in detail.]{.c4}

# [The big picture]{.calibre} {#Chapter9LearningandAdaptation.xhtml_h.x9ozkpr4fv6l .c5}

[Agents learn and adapt by changing their thinking, actions, or
knowledge based on new experiences and data.]{.c4} [ ]{.c4} [This allows
agents to evolve from simply following instructions to becoming smarter
over time.]{.c4}

- [Reinforcement Learning:]{.c8} [ Agents try actions and receive
  rewards for positive outcomes and penalties for negative ones,
  learning optimal behaviors in changing situations.]{.c9} [ ]{.c9}
  [Useful for agents controlling robots or playing games.]{.c9}
- [Supervised Learning:]{.c8} [ Agents learn from labeled examples,
  connecting inputs to desired outputs, enabling tasks like
  decision-making and pattern recognition.]{.c9} [ ]{.c9} [Ideal for
  agents sorting emails or predicting trends.]{.c9}
- [Unsupervised Learning:]{.c8} [ Agents discover hidden connections and
  patterns in unlabeled data, aiding in insights, organization, and
  creating a mental map of their environment.]{.c9} [ ]{.c9} [Useful for
  agents exploring data without specific guidance.]{.c9}
- [Few-Shot/Zero-Shot Learning with LLM-Based Agents:]{.c8} [ Agents
  leveraging LLMs can quickly adapt to new tasks with minimal examples
  or clear instructions, enabling rapid responses to new commands or
  situations.]{.c9}
- [Online Learning:]{.c8} [ Agents continuously update knowledge with
  new data, essential for real-time reactions and ongoing adaptation in
  dynamic environments.]{.c9} [ ]{.c9} [Critical for agents processing
  continuous data streams.]{.c9}
- [Memory-Based Learning:]{.c8} [ Agents recall past experiences to
  adjust current actions in similar situations, enhancing context
  awareness and decision-making.]{.c9} [ ]{.c9} [Effective for agents
  with memory recall capabilities.]{.c9}

[Agents adapt by changing strategy, understanding, or goals based on
learning.]{.c11} [ ]{.c11} [This is vital for agents in unpredictable,
changing, or new environments.]{.c11}

[Proximal Policy Optimization (PPO)]{.c13} [ ]{.c4} [is a reinforcement
learning algorithm used to train agents in environments with a
continuous range of actions, like controlling a robot\'s joints or a
character in a game. Its main goal is to reliably and stably improve an
agent\'s decision-making strategy, known as its policy.]{.c14}

[The core idea behind PPO is to make small, careful updates to the
agent\'s policy. It avoids drastic changes that could cause performance
to collapse. Here\'s how it works:]{.c14}

1.  [Collect Data: The agent interacts with its environment (e.g., plays
    a game) using its current policy and collects a batch of experiences
    (state, action, reward).]{.c14}
2.  [Evaluate a \"Surrogate\" Goal: PPO calculates how a potential
    policy update would change the expected reward. However, instead of
    just maximizing this reward, it uses a special \"clipped\" objective
    function.]{.c14}
3.  [The \"Clipping\" Mechanism: This is the key to PPO\'s stability. It
    creates a \"trust region\" or a safe zone around the current policy.
    The algorithm is prevented from making an update that is too
    different from the current strategy. This clipping acts like a
    safety brake, ensuring the agent doesn\'t take a huge, risky step
    that undoes its learning.]{.c14}

[In short, PPO balances improving performance with staying close to a
known, working strategy, which prevents catastrophic failures during
training and leads to more stable learning.]{.c14}

[Direct Preference Optimization (DPO)]{.c13} [ is a more recent method
designed specifically for aligning Large Language Models (LLMs) with
human preferences. It offers a simpler, more direct alternative to using
PPO for this task.]{.c14}

[To understand DPO, it helps to first understand the traditional
PPO-based alignment method:]{.c14}

- [The PPO Approach (Two-Step Process):]{.c14}

1.  [Train a Reward Model: First, you collect human feedback data where
    people rate or compare different LLM responses (e.g., \"Response A
    is better than Response B\"). This data is used to train a separate
    AI model, called a reward model, whose job is to predict what score
    a human would give to any new response.]{.c14}
2.  [Fine-Tune with PPO: Next, the LLM is fine-tuned using PPO. The
    LLM\'s goal is to generate responses that get the highest possible
    score from the reward model. The reward model acts as the \"judge\"
    in the training game.]{.c14}

[This two-step process can be complex and unstable. For instance, the
LLM might find a loophole and learn to \"hack\" the reward model to get
high scores for bad responses.]{.c14}

- [The DPO Approach (Direct Process): DPO skips the reward model
  entirely. Instead of translating human preferences into a reward score
  and then optimizing for that score, DPO uses the preference data
  directly to update the LLM\'s policy.]{.c14}
- [It works by using a mathematical relationship that directly links
  preference data to the optimal policy. It essentially teaches the
  model: \"Increase the probability of generating responses like the
  ]{.c4} [preferred]{.c20} [ one and decrease the probability of
  generating ones like the ]{.c4} [disfavored]{.c20} [ one.\"]{.c14}

[In essence, DPO simplifies alignment by directly optimizing the
language model on human preference data. This avoids the complexity and
potential instability of training and using a separate reward model,
making the alignment process more efficient and robust.]{.c14}

# [Practical Applications & Use Cases]{.calibre} {#Chapter9LearningandAdaptation.xhtml_h.b6vlwj6z509e .c5}

[Adaptive agents exhibit enhanced performance in variable environments
through iterative updates driven by experiential data.]{.c4}

- [Personalized assistant agents]{.c8} [ refine interaction protocols
  through longitudinal analysis of individual user behaviors, ensuring
  highly optimized response generation.]{.c9}
- [Trading bot agents]{.c8} [ optimize decision-making algorithms by
  dynamically adjusting model parameters based on high-resolution,
  real-time market data, thereby maximizing financial returns and
  mitigating risk factors.]{.c9}
- [Application agents]{.c8} [ optimize user interface and functionality
  through dynamic modification based on observed user behavior,
  resulting in increased user engagement and system intuitiveness.]{.c9}
- [Robotic and autonomous vehicle agents]{.c8} [ enhance navigation and
  response capabilities by integrating sensor data and historical action
  analysis, enabling safe and efficient operation across diverse
  environmental conditions.]{.c9}
- [Fraud detection agents]{.c8} [ improve anomaly detection by refining
  predictive models with newly identified fraudulent patterns, enhancing
  system security and minimizing financial losses.]{.c9}
- [Recommendation agents]{.c8} [ improve content selection precision by
  employing user preference learning algorithms, providing highly
  individualized and contextually relevant recommendations.]{.c9}
- [Game AI agents]{.c8} [ enhance player engagement by dynamically
  adapting strategic algorithms, thereby increasing game complexity and
  challenge.]{.c9}
- [Knowledge Base Learning Agents]{.c8} [:]{.c9} [ Agents can leverage
  Retrieval Augmented Generation (RAG) to maintain a dynamic knowledge
  base of problem descriptions and proven solutions (see the Chapter
  14).]{.c9} [ ]{.c9} [By storing successful strategies and challenges
  encountered, the agent can reference this data during decision-making,
  enabling it to adapt to new situations more effectively by applying
  previously successful patterns or avoiding known pitfalls.]{.c9}

# [Case Study: The Self-Improving Coding Agent (SICA)]{.calibre} {#Chapter9LearningandAdaptation.xhtml_h.8so34wgg8rnx .c5}

[The Self-Improving Coding Agent (SICA), developed by Maxime Robeyns,
Laurence Aitchison, and Martin Szummer, represents an advancement in
agent-based learning, demonstrating the capacity for an agent to modify
its own source code.]{.c4} [ ]{.c4} [This contrasts with traditional
approaches where one agent might train another; SICA acts as both the
modifier and the modified entity, iteratively refining its code base to
improve performance across various coding challenges.]{.c4}

[SICA\'s self-improvement operates through an iterative cycle (see
Fig.1).]{.c4} [ ]{.c4} [Initially, SICA reviews an archive of its past
versions and their performance on benchmark tests.]{.c4} [ ]{.c4} [It
selects the version with the highest performance score, calculated based
on a weighted formula considering success, time, and computational
cost.]{.c4} [ ]{.c4} [This selected version then undertakes the next
round of self-modification.]{.c4} [ ]{.c4} [It analyzes the archive to
identify potential improvements and then directly alters its
codebase.]{.c4} [ ]{.c4} [The modified agent is subsequently tested
against benchmarks, with the results recorded in the archive.]{.c4}
[ ]{.c4} [This process repeats, facilitating learning directly from past
performance.]{.c4} [ T]{.c4} [his self-improvement mechanism allows SICA
to evolve its capabilities without requiring traditional training ]{.c4}
[paradigms.]{.c4}

[ ![](15/GoogleDoc/images/image1.png){.calibre2} ]{.calibre1}

[Fig.1: SICA\'s self-improvement, learning and adapting based on its
past versions]{.c14}

[]{.c24}

[SICA underwent significant self-improvement, leading to advancements in
code editing and navigation.]{.c4} [ ]{.c4} [Initially, SICA utilized a
basic file-overwriting approach for code changes.]{.c4} [ ]{.c4} [It
subsequently developed a \"Smart Editor\" capable of more intelligent
and contextual edits.]{.c4} [ ]{.c4} [This evolved into a
\"Diff-Enhanced Smart Editor,\" incorporating diffs for targeted
modifications and pattern-based editing, and a \"Quick Overwrite Tool\"
to reduce processing demands.]{.c4} [ ]{.c14}

[SICA further implemented \"Minimal Diff Output Optimization\" and
\"Context-Sensitive Diff Minimization,\" using Abstract Syntax Tree
(AST) parsing for efficiency.]{.c4} [ ]{.c4} [Additionally, a
\"SmartEditor Input Normalizer\" was added.]{.c4} [ ]{.c4} [In terms of
navigation, SICA independently created an \"AST Symbol Locator,\" using
the code\'s structural map (AST) to identify definitions within the
codebase.]{.c4} [ ]{.c4} [Later, a \"Hybrid Symbol Locator\" was
developed, combining a quick search with AST checking.]{.c4} [ ]{.c4}
[This was further optimized via \"Optimized AST Parsing in Hybrid Symbol
Locator\" to focus on relevant code sections, improving search
speed.]{.c4} [(see Fig. 2)]{.c14}

[ ![](15/GoogleDoc/images/image4.png){.calibre4} ]{.calibre3}

[Fig.2 : Performance across iterations. Key improvements are annotated
with their corresponding tool or agent modifications. (courtesy of
Maxime Robeyns , Martin Szummer , Laurence Aitchison)]{.c4}

[SICA\'s architecture comprises a foundational toolkit for basic file
operations, command execution, and arithmetic calculations.]{.c4}
[ ]{.c4} [It includes mechanisms for result submission and the
invocation of specialized sub-agents (coding, problem-solving, and
reasoning).]{.c4} [ ]{.c4} [These sub-agents decompose complex tasks and
manage the LLM\'s context length, especially during extended improvement
cycles.]{.c4} [ ]{.c14}

[An asynchronous overseer, another LLM, monitors SICA\'s behavior,
identifying potential issues such as loops or stagnation.]{.c4} [ ]{.c4}
[It communicates with SICA and can intervene to halt execution if
necessary.]{.c4} [ ]{.c4} [The overseer receives a detailed report of
SICA\'s actions, including a callgraph and a log of messages and tool
actions, to identify patterns and inefficiencies.]{.c4}

[SICA\'s LLM organizes information within its context window, its
short-term memory, in a structured manner crucial to its
operation.]{.c4} [ ]{.c4} [This structure includes a System Prompt
defining agent goals, tool and sub-agent documentation, and system
instructions.]{.c4} [ ]{.c4} [A Core Prompt contains the problem
statement or instruction, content of open files, and a directory
map.]{.c4} [ ]{.c4} [Assistant Messages record the agent\'s step-by-step
reasoning, tool and sub-agent call records and results, and overseer
communications.]{.c4} [ ]{.c4} [This organization facilitates efficient
information flow, enhancing LLM operation and reducing processing time
and costs.]{.c4} [ ]{.c4} [Initially, file changes were recorded as
diffs, showing only modifications and periodically consolidated.]{.c4}

[SICA: A Look at the Code: ]{.c13} [Delving deeper into SICA\'s
implementation reveals several key design choices that underpin its
capabilities. As discussed, the system is built with a ]{.c4} [modular
architecture]{.c4} [, incorporating several sub-agents, such as a coding
agent, a problem-solver agent, and a reasoning agent. These sub-agents
are invoked by the main agent, much like tool calls, serving to
decompose complex tasks and efficiently manage context length,
especially during those extended meta-improvement iterations. ]{.c14}

[The project is actively developed and aims to provide a robust
framework for those interested in post-training LLMs on tool use and
other agentic tasks, with the full code available for further
exploration and contribution at the ]{.c4} [
[https://github.com/MaximeRobeyns/self_improving_coding_agent/](https://www.google.com/url?q=https://github.com/MaximeRobeyns/self_improving_coding_agent/&sa=D&source=editors&ust=1759228445714155&usg=AOvVaw0XKJ11tE-y2SL1vq9gFLz7){.c26}
]{.c25} [ GitHub repository.]{.c14}

[For security, the project strongly emphasizes ]{.c4} [Docker
containerization]{.c4} [, meaning the agent runs within a dedicated
Docker container. This is a crucial measure, as it provides isolation
from the host machine, mitigating risks like inadvertent file system
manipulation given the agent\'s ability to execute shell commands.
]{.c14}

[To ensure transparency and control, the system features robust ]{.c4}
[observability]{.c4} [ through an interactive webpage that visualizes
events on the event bus and the agent\'s callgraph. This offers
comprehensive insights into the agent\'s actions, allowing users to
inspect individual events, read overseer messages, and collapse
sub-agent traces for clearer understanding. ]{.c14}

[In terms of its core intelligence, the agent framework supports ]{.c4}
[LLM integration]{.c4} [ from various providers, enabling
experimentation with different models to find the best fit for specific
tasks. Finally, a critical component is the ]{.c4} [asynchronous
overseer]{.c4} [, an LLM that runs concurrently with the main agent.
This overseer periodically assesses the agent\'s behavior for
pathological deviations or stagnation and can intervene by sending
notifications or even cancelling the agent\'s execution if necessary. It
receives a detailed textual representation of the system\'s state,
including a callgraph and an event stream of LLM messages, tool calls,
and responses, which allows it to detect inefficient patterns or
repeated work. ]{.c14}

[A notable challenge in the initial SICA implementation was prompting
the LLM-based agent to independently propose novel, innovative,
feasible, and engaging modifications during each meta-improvement
iteration.]{.c14} [ ]{.c14} [This limitation, particularly in fostering
open-ended learning and authentic creativity in LLM agents, remains a
key area of investigation in current research.]{.c14}

# [AlphaEvolve and OpenEvolve]{.c28} {#Chapter9LearningandAdaptation.xhtml_h.2v7t6xkandg7 .c27}

[AlphaEvolve]{.c8} [ is an AI agent developed by Google designed to
discover and optimize algorithms.]{.c9} [ ]{.c9} [It utilizes a
combination of LLMs, specifically Gemini models (Flash and Pro),
automated evaluation systems, and an evolutionary algorithm
framework.]{.c9} [ ]{.c9} [This system aims to advance both theoretical
mathematics and practical computing applications.]{.c9}

[]{.c11}

[AlphaEvolve employs an ensemble of Gemini models.]{.c9} [ ]{.c9} [Flash
is used for generating a wide range of initial algorithm proposals,
while Pro provides more in-depth analysis and refinement.]{.c9} [ ]{.c9}
[Proposed algorithms are then automatically evaluated and scored based
on predefined criteria.]{.c9} [ ]{.c9} [This evaluation provides
feedback that is used to iteratively improve the solutions, leading to
optimized and novel algorithms.]{.c9}

[]{.c11}

[In practical computing, AlphaEvolve has been deployed within Google\'s
infrastructure.]{.c9} [ ]{.c9} [It has demonstrated improvements in data
center scheduling, resulting in a 0.7% reduction in global compute
resource usage.]{.c9} [ ]{.c9} [It has also contributed to hardware
design by suggesting optimizations for Verilog code in upcoming Tensor
Processing Units (TPUs).]{.c9} [ ]{.c9} [Furthermore, AlphaEvolve has
accelerated AI performance, including a 23% speed improvement in a core
kernel of the Gemini architecture and up to 32.5% optimization of
low-level GPU instructions for FlashAttention.]{.c9}

[]{.c11}

[In the realm of fundamental research, AlphaEvolve has contributed to
the discovery of new algorithms for matrix multiplication, including a
method for 4x4 complex-valued matrices that uses 48 scalar
multiplications, surpassing previously known solutions.]{.c9} [ ]{.c9}
[In broader mathematical research, it has rediscovered existing
state-of-the-art solutions to over 50 open problems in 75% of cases and
improved upon existing solutions in 20% of cases, with examples
including advancements in the kissing number problem.]{.c9}

[]{.c11}

[OpenEvolve]{.c8} [ is an evolutionary coding agent that leverages LLMs
(see Fig.3) to iteratively optimize code. It orchestrates a pipeline of
LLM-driven code generation, evaluation, and selection to continuously
enhance programs for a wide range of tasks. A key aspect of OpenEvolve
is its capability to evolve entire code files, rather than being limited
to single functions. The agent is designed for versatility, offering
support for multiple programming languages and compatibility with
OpenAI-compatible APIs for any LLM. Furthermore, it incorporates
multi-objective optimization, allows for flexible prompt engineering,
and is capable of distributed evaluation to efficiently handle complex
coding challenges. ]{.c11}

[]{.c31}

[ ![](15/GoogleDoc/images/image2.png){.calibre2} ]{.calibre1}

[Fig. 3: ]{.c9} [The OpenEvolve internal architecture is managed by a
controller.]{.c11} [ ]{.c11} [This controller orchestrates several key
components: the program sampler, Program Database, Evaluator Pool, and
LLM Ensembles.]{.c11} [ ]{.c11} [Its primary function is to facilitate
their learning and adaptation processes to enhance code quality.]{.c11}

[]{.c11}

[This code snippet uses the OpenEvolve library to perform evolutionary
optimization on a program. It initializes the OpenEvolve system with
paths to an initial program, an evaluation file, and a configuration
file. The evolve.run(iterations=1000) line starts the evolutionary
process, running for 1000 iterations to find an improved version of the
program. Finally, it prints the metrics of the best program found during
the evolution, formatted to four decimal places. ]{.c11}

[]{.c11}

+-----------------------------------------------------------------------+
| [from openevolve import OpenEvolve]{.c37}                             |
|                                                                       |
| []{.c37}                                                              |
|                                                                       |
| [\# Initialize the system]{.c37}                                      |
|                                                                       |
| [evolve = OpenEvolve(]{.c37}                                          |
|                                                                       |
| [   initial_program_path=\"path/to/initial_program.py\",]{.c37}       |
|                                                                       |
| [   evaluation_file=\"path/to/evaluator.py\",]{.c37}                  |
|                                                                       |
| [   config_path=\"path/to/config.yaml\"]{.c37}                        |
|                                                                       |
| [)]{.c37}                                                             |
|                                                                       |
| []{.c37}                                                              |
|                                                                       |
| [\# Run the evolution]{.c37}                                          |
|                                                                       |
| [best_program = await evolve.run(iterations=1000)]{.c37}              |
|                                                                       |
| [print(f\"Best program metrics:\")]{.c37}                             |
|                                                                       |
| [for name, value in best_program.metrics.items():]{.c37}              |
|                                                                       |
| [   print(f\"  {name}: {value:.4f}\")]{.c37}                          |
+-----------------------------------------------------------------------+

[]{.c2}

# [At a Glance]{.c28} {#Chapter9LearningandAdaptation.xhtml_h.ief9g1nmcym3 .c5}

[What: ]{.c8} [AI agents often operate in dynamic and unpredictable
environments where pre-programmed logic is insufficient. Their
performance can degrade when faced with novel situations not anticipated
during their initial design. Without the ability to learn from
experience, agents cannot optimize their strategies or personalize their
interactions over time. This rigidity limits their effectiveness and
prevents them from achieving true autonomy in complex, real-world
scenarios.]{.c11}

[Why: ]{.c39} [The standardized solution is to integrate learning and
adaptation mechanisms, transforming static agents into dynamic, evolving
systems. This allows an agent to autonomously refine its knowledge and
behaviors based on new data and interactions. Agentic systems can use
various methods, from reinforcement learning to more advanced techniques
like self-modification, as seen in the Self-Improving Coding Agent
(SICA). Advanced systems like Google\'s AlphaEvolve leverage LLMs and
evolutionary algorithms to discover entirely new and more efficient
solutions to complex problems. By continuously learning, agents can
master new tasks, enhance their performance, and adapt to changing
conditions without requiring constant manual reprogramming.]{.c40}

[Rule of thumb: ]{.c8} [Use this pattern when building agents that must
operate in dynamic, uncertain, or evolving environments. It is essential
for applications requiring personalization, continuous performance
improvement, and the ability to handle novel situations
autonomously.]{.c11}

[Visual summary]{.c41}

[ ![](15/GoogleDoc/images/image3.png){.calibre2} ]{.calibre1}

[Fig.4: Learning and adapting pattern]{.c11}

[]{.c2}

# [Key Takeaways]{.c28} {#Chapter9LearningandAdaptation.xhtml_h.bw2bc0p521wg .c5}

- [Learning and Adaptation are about agents getting better at what they
  do and handling new situations by using their experiences.]{.c14}
- [\"Adaptation\" is the visible change in an agent\'s behavior or
  knowledge that comes from learning.]{.c14}
- [SICA, the Self-Improving Coding Agent, self-improves by modifying its
  code based on past performance.]{.c14} [ ]{.c14} [This led to tools
  like the Smart Editor and AST Symbol Locator.]{.c14}
- [Having specialized \"sub-agents\" and an \"overseer\" helps these
  self-improving systems manage big tasks and stay on track.]{.c14}
- [The way an LLM\'s \"context window\" is set up (with system prompts,
  core prompts, and assistant messages) is super important for how
  efficiently agents work.]{.c14}
- [This pattern is vital for agents that need to operate in environments
  that are always changing, uncertain, or require a personal
  touch.]{.c14}
- [Building agents that learn often means hooking them up with machine
  learning tools and managing how data flows.]{.c14}
- [An agent system, equipped with basic coding tools, can autonomously
  edit itself, and thereby improve its performance on benchmark
  tasks]{.c14}
- [AlphaEvolve is Google\'s AI agent that leverages LLMs and an
  evolutionary framework to autonomously discover and optimize
  algorithms, significantly enhancing both fundamental research and
  practical computing applications.]{.c4} [.]{.c14}

# [Conclusion]{.calibre} {#Chapter9LearningandAdaptation.xhtml_h.srioeptcfh1n .c44}

[This chapter examines the crucial roles of learning and adaptation in
Artificial Intelligence.]{.c14} [ ]{.c14} [AI agents enhance their
performance through continuous data acquisition and experience.]{.c14}
[ ]{.c14} [The Self-Improving Coding Agent (SICA) exemplifies this by
autonomously improving its capabilities through code
modifications.]{.c14}

[]{.c14}

[We have reviewed the fundamental components of agentic AI, including
architecture, applications, planning, multi-agent collaboration, memory
management, and learning and adaptation.]{.c14} [ ]{.c14} [Learning
principles are particularly vital for coordinated improvement in
multi-agent systems.]{.c14} [ ]{.c14} [To achieve this, tuning data must
accurately reflect the complete interaction trajectory, capturing the
individual inputs and outputs of each participating agent.]{.c14}

[]{.c14}

[These elements contribute to significant advancements, such as
Google\'s AlphaEvolve.]{.c14} [ ]{.c14} [This AI system independently
discovers and refines algorithms by LLMs, automated assessment, and an
evolutionary approach, driving progress in scientific research and
computational techniques.]{.c14} [ ]{.c14} [Such patterns can be
combined to construct sophisticated AI systems.]{.c14} [ ]{.c14}
[Developments like AlphaEvolve demonstrate that autonomous algorithmic
discovery and optimization by AI agents are attainable.]{.c14}
