[]{#IndexofTerms_split_001.xhtml}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

# []{.c5} {#IndexofTerms_split_001.xhtml_h.pu6c7rj4rg19 .c11}

# [Prompt]{.c5} {#IndexofTerms_split_001.xhtml_h.8m8xyxtq27op .c12}

[Below is the prompt that was utilized to create this index of
terms.]{.c5}

[]{.c5}

+----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------+
| [do you read                                                                                 |
| https://docs.google.com/document/d/1rsaK53T3Lg5KoGwvf8ukOUvbELRtH-V0LnOIFDxBryE/edit?tab=t.0 |
| and all the related documents in the first page]{.c16}                                       |
|                                                                                              |
| [Please extract all the titles for each chapter. and print them.]{.c16}                      |
|                                                                                              |
| [Then process the content of all the individual chapters, and create a detailed index used   |
| for publishing, a list of words or phrases (\'headings\') and associated pointers            |
| (\'locators\') to where useful material relating to that heading can be found in a document  |
| or collection of documents. I don\'t want a sample index. I want the full index, with 100    |
| index terms]{.c16}                                                                           |
+----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------+

## [Gemini 2.5: Reasoning Steps]{.c5} {#IndexofTerms_split_001.xhtml_h.tur3pbvrc4sg .c17}

[Beginning Index Creation]{.c18}

[I\'ve initiated the process by reading the Google Doc provided.
Currently, I\'m focusing on understanding the user\'s request:
extracting chapter titles to build a detailed index. I\'m carefully
reviewing the document, looking for clear structural elements that would
indicate chapter breaks. I\'m hoping to identify consistent formatting
or keywords that will guide the indexing process.]{.c19}

[Defining Index Extraction Process]{.c18}

[I\'m now fully immersed in the task. I\'ve broken down the user\'s
request into actionable steps. My initial focus is using ]{.c20}
[Workspaces]{.c20} [ to access the main Google Doc. Following this,
I\'ll parse the document to locate and retrieve related documents, which
will also be accessed using the same tool. Finally, I will extract all
chapter titles from all the documents, and create a comprehensive index.
I\'ve planned the detailed process.]{.c19}

[Refining the Extraction Strategy]{.c18}

[The user\'s instructions are clear: read a Google Doc, its related
documents, and build a detailed index of chapter titles. I\'ve devised a
multi-step plan. First, ]{.c20} [Workspaces]{.c20} [ will access the
main Google Doc. Then, I\'ll parse it to find and read related
documents, also using ]{.c20} [Workspaces]{.c20} [. I\'m now focusing on
extracting titles and forming the detailed index with the required
terms. I\'m prioritizing accuracy to meet the user\'s specific demands
for a comprehensive index.]{.c19}

[1 . Establish user intent:]{.c18}

[I\'ve determined the user wants a chapter title list and comprehensive
index from the Google Doc and related documents. Now I need to figure
out the most effective method for this extraction and indexing process.
I\'m exploring potential API functionalities to streamline this task,
and will need to assess the best strategy for handling different
document structures.]{.c19}

[3. Considering Tool Use]{.c18}

[I\'ve extracted the chapter URLs from the main document using a regular
expression. Now, I\'m ready to utilize ]{.c20} [Workspaces]{.c20} [ to
retrieve the actual content of each chapter. Once I have the text for
each chapter, I\'ll proceed with indexing.]{.c19}

[Accessing chapter content:]{.c18}

[I\'ve successfully identified the URLs for each chapter from the main
document, which I can now use with ]{.c20} [Workspaces]{.c20} [. My next
step is to retrieve the content from these URLs, which will then allow
me to generate the index. I\'m focusing on efficiency, aiming to process
this information quickly.]{.c19}

[Fetching Chapter Content]{.c18}

[I\'ve successfully identified the chapter URLs from the main document
and am ready to utilize ]{.c20} [Workspaces]{.c20} [. My plan involves
extracting these URLs, batching the calls for efficiency, and then
processing the retrieved content to create the desired index. This
ensures all relevant information is captured, paving the way for a
comprehensive and accurate result.]{.c19}

[Initiating URL Retrieval]{.c18}

[I\'ve now successfully extracted the chapter URLs from the main
document. Currently, I\'m setting up calls to the ]{.c20}
[Workspaces]{.c20} [ tool, preparing to fetch the content of each
chapter. The goal is to obtain the necessary text for indexing. This
information will then facilitate the creation of the index with at least
100 terms as requested.]{.c19}

[Initiating Chapter Extraction]{.c18}

[I\'ve successfully identified and extracted the chapter URLs from the
main document, which I\'ve included above. I am now proceeding to use
]{.c20} [Workspaces]{.c20} [ for each URL to obtain the chapter content.
The goal is to obtain the necessary text for indexing. This information
will then facilitate the creation of the index with at least 100 terms
as requested. I\'ve begun to retrieve this information.]{.c19}

[Orchestrating Chapter Retrieval]{.c18}

[I\'ve extracted the chapter URLs and initiated a call to ]{.c20}
[Workspaces]{.c20} [. I\'m currently retrieving the content for each
chapter. To ensure a smooth process, I\'m batching the calls to prevent
any rate-limiting issues. This should enable me to efficiently compile
the text needed for the index.]{.c19}

[Decoding User Request]{.c18}

[I\'ve determined the user\'s need. They require two primary
deliverables: first, a comprehensive list of chapter titles extracted
from Google Docs, and second, a detailed index containing a minimum of
one hundred key terms. The process of extracting titles seems
straightforward, while curating the index will involve more nuanced
analysis.]{.c19}

[Extracting Titles and Indexing]{.c18}

[I\'ve successfully identified the user\'s need: extracting chapter
titles from the provided documents and compiling a comprehensive index.
I\'ve fetched the content of 31 Google Docs, including the main document
and its chapters. I\'ve confirmed that no additional tools are needed.
My plan involves two steps: first, extracting chapter titles from the
document filenames, and second, meticulously processing the text to
build the index with key terms and chapter locators.]{.c19}

[Developing an Extraction Plan]{.c18}

[I\'ve refined my approach to tackle the user\'s request. I\'ve now
formulated a precise plan. Firstly, I will compile the chapter titles
from the filenames of the 31 fetched documents. Secondly, and more
complexly, I will meticulously analyze the content to create a
comprehensive, alphabetically ordered index exceeding 100 entries. I
have finalized my extraction and indexing strategy and can now commence
execution.]{.c19}

[Crafting the Extraction Strategy]{.c18}

[My approach is well-defined. I will compile chapter titles from the
filenames of the 31 fetched documents. Subsequently, I\'ll analyze the
content meticulously to construct an alphabetically organized index
exceeding 100 entries. I\'ve finalized the extraction and indexing
strategy and can now commence implementation. The user\'s goal of a
detailed index, covering content from diverse chapters and appendices
within the Agentic Design Patterns series, is now within reach. I\'ll
focus on delivering a thorough response, going beyond a mere sample
index.]{.c19}

[Defining the Approach]{.c18}

[I\'ve clarified the user\'s need. I will extract chapter titles from
the document filenames. Following that, I will carefully create an index
exceeding 100 entries, arranging them alphabetically and noting their
chapter locations. I am prepared to begin the indexing and extraction
process.]{.c19}

[]{.c5}

[]{#OnlinecontributionFrequentlyAskedQuestionsAgenticDesignPatterns.xhtml}

### [Frequently Asked Questions: Agentic Design Patterns]{.c2} {#OnlinecontributionFrequentlyAskedQuestionsAgenticDesignPatterns.xhtml_h.ju1jr4grojqi .c1}

[What is an \"agentic design pattern\"?]{.c4} [ An agentic design
pattern is a reusable, high-level solution to a common problem
encountered when building intelligent, autonomous systems (agents).
These patterns provide a structured framework for designing agent
behaviors, much like software design patterns do for traditional
programming. They help developers build more robust, predictable, and
effective AI agents.]{.c5}

[What is the main goal of this guide?]{.c4} [ The guide aims to provide
a practical, hands-on introduction to designing and building agentic
systems. It moves beyond theoretical discussions to offer concrete
architectural blueprints that developers can use to create agents
capable of complex, goal-oriented behavior in a reliable way.]{.c5}

[Who is the intended audience for this guide?]{.c4} [ This guide is
written for AI developers, software engineers, and system architects who
are building applications with large language models (LLMs) and other AI
components. It is for those who want to move from simple prompt-response
interactions to creating sophisticated, autonomous agents.]{.c5}

[4. What are some of the key agentic patterns discussed?]{.c4} [ Based
on the table of contents, the guide covers several key patterns,
including:]{.c5}

- [Reflection:]{.c4} [ The ability of an agent to critique its own
  actions and outputs to improve performance.]{.c5}
- [Planning:]{.c4} [ The process of breaking down a complex goal into
  smaller, manageable steps or tasks.]{.c5}
- [Tool Use:]{.c4} [ The pattern of an agent utilizing external tools
  (like code interpreters, search engines, or other APIs) to acquire
  information or perform actions it cannot do on its own.]{.c5}
- [Multi-Agent Collaboration:]{.c4} [ The architecture for having
  multiple specialized agents work together to solve a problem, often
  involving a \"leader\" or \"orchestrator\" agent.]{.c5}
- [Human-in-the-Loop:]{.c4} [ The integration of human oversight and
  intervention, allowing for feedback, correction, and approval of an
  agent\'s actions.]{.c5}

[Why is \"planning\" an important pattern?]{.c4} [ Planning is crucial
because it allows an agent to tackle complex, multi-step tasks that
cannot be solved with a single action. By creating a plan, the agent can
maintain a coherent strategy, track its progress, and handle errors or
unexpected obstacles in a structured manner. This prevents the agent
from getting \"stuck\" or deviating from the user\'s ultimate
goal.]{.c5}

[What is the difference between a \"tool\" and a \"skill\" for an
agent?]{.c4} [ While the terms are often used interchangeably, a
\"tool\" generally refers to an external resource the agent can call
upon (e.g., a weather API, a calculator). A \"skill\" is a more
integrated capability that the agent has learned, often combining tool
use with internal reasoning to perform a specific function (e.g., the
skill of \"booking a flight\" might involve using calendar and airline
APIs).]{.c5}

[How does the \"Reflection\" pattern improve an agent\'s
performance?]{.c4} [ Reflection acts as a form of self-correction. After
generating a response or completing a task, the agent can be prompted to
review its work, check for errors, assess its quality against certain
criteria, or consider alternative approaches. This iterative refinement
process helps the agent produce more accurate, relevant, and
high-quality results.]{.c5}

[What is the core idea of the Reflection pattern?]{.c4} [ The Reflection
pattern gives an agent the ability to step back and critique its own
work. Instead of producing a final output in one go, the agent generates
a draft and then \"reflects\" on it, identifying flaws, missing
information, or areas for improvement. This self-correction process is
key to enhancing the quality and accuracy of its responses.]{.c5}

[Why is simple \"prompt chaining\" not enough for high-quality
output?]{.c4} [ Simple prompt chaining (where the output of one prompt
becomes the input for the next) is often too basic. The model might just
rephrase its previous output without genuinely improving it. A true
Reflection pattern requires a more structured critique, prompting the
agent to analyze its work against specific standards, check for logical
errors, or verify facts.]{.c5}

[What are the two main types of reflection mentioned in this
chapter?]{.c4} [ The chapter discusses two primary forms of
reflection:]{.c5}

- [\"Check your work\" Reflection:]{.c4} [ This is a basic form where
  the agent is simply asked to review and fix its previous output. It\'s
  a good starting point for catching simple errors.]{.c5}
- [\"Internal Critic\" Reflection:]{.c4} [ This is a more advanced form
  where a separate, \"critic\" agent (or a dedicated prompt) is used to
  evaluate the output of the \"worker\" agent. This critic can be given
  specific criteria to look for, leading to more rigorous and targeted
  improvements.]{.c5}

[How does reflection help in reducing \"hallucinations\"?]{.c4} [ By
prompting an agent to review its work, especially by comparing its
statements against a known source or by checking its own reasoning
steps, the Reflection pattern can significantly reduce the likelihood of
hallucinations (making up facts). The agent is forced to be more
grounded in the provided context and less likely to generate unsupported
information.]{.c5}

[Can the Reflection pattern be applied more than once?]{.c4} [ Yes,
reflection can be an iterative process. An agent can be made to reflect
on its work multiple times, with each loop refining the output further.
This is particularly useful for complex tasks where the first or second
attempt may still contain subtle errors or could be substantially
improved.]{.c5}

[What is the Planning pattern in the context of AI agents?]{.c4} [ The
Planning pattern involves enabling an agent to break down a complex,
high-level goal into a sequence of smaller, actionable steps. Instead of
trying to solve a big problem at once, the agent first creates a
\"plan\" and then executes each step in the plan, which is a much more
reliable approach.]{.c5}

[Why is planning necessary for complex tasks?]{.c4} [ LLMs can struggle
with tasks that require multiple steps or dependencies. Without a plan,
an agent might lose track of the overall objective, miss crucial steps,
or fail to handle the output of one step as the input for the next. A
plan provides a clear roadmap, ensuring all requirements of the original
request are met in a logical order.]{.c5}

[What is a common way to implement the Planning pattern?]{.c4} [ A
common implementation is to have the agent first generate a list of
steps in a structured format (like a JSON array or a numbered list). The
system can then iterate through this list, executing each step one by
one and feeding the result back to the agent to inform the next
action.]{.c5}

[How does the agent handle errors or changes during execution?]{.c4} [ A
robust planning pattern allows for dynamic adjustments. If a step fails
or the situation changes, the agent can be prompted to \"re-plan\" from
the current state. It can analyze the error, modify the remaining steps,
or even add new ones to overcome the obstacle.]{.c5}

[Does the user see the plan?]{.c4} [ This is a design choice. In many
cases, showing the plan to the user first for approval is a great
practice. This aligns with the \"Human-in-the-Loop\" pattern, giving the
user transparency and control over the agent\'s proposed actions before
they are executed.]{.c5}

[What does the \"Tool Use\" pattern entail?]{.c4} [ The Tool Use pattern
allows an agent to extend its capabilities by interacting with external
software or APIs. Since an LLM\'s knowledge is static and it can\'t
perform real-world actions on its own, tools give it access to live
information (e.g., Google Search), proprietary data (e.g., a company\'s
database), or the ability to perform actions (e.g., send an email, book
a meeting).]{.c5}

[How does an agent decide which tool to use?]{.c4} [ The agent is
typically given a list of available tools along with descriptions of
what each tool does and what parameters it requires. When faced with a
request it can\'t handle with its internal knowledge, the agent\'s
reasoning ability allows it to select the most appropriate tool from the
list to accomplish the task.]{.c5}

[What is the \"ReAct\" (Reason and Act) framework mentioned in this
context?]{.c4} [ ReAct is a popular framework that integrates reasoning
and acting. The agent follows a loop of ]{.calibre} [Thought]{.c4}
[ (reasoning about what it needs to do), ]{.calibre} [Action]{.c4}
[ (deciding which tool to use and with what inputs), and ]{.calibre}
[Observation]{.c4} [ (seeing the result from the tool). This loop
continues until it has gathered enough information to fulfill the
user\'s request.]{.c5}

[What are some challenges in implementing tool use?]{.c4} [ Key
challenges include:]{.c5}

- [Error Handling:]{.c4} [ Tools can fail, return unexpected data, or
  time out. The agent needs to be able to recognize these errors and
  decide whether to try again, use a different tool, or ask the user for
  help.]{.c5}
- [Security:]{.c4} [ Giving an agent access to tools, especially those
  that perform actions, has security implications. It\'s crucial to have
  safeguards, permissions, and often human approval for sensitive
  operations.]{.c5}
- [Prompting:]{.c4} [ The agent must be prompted effectively to generate
  correctly formatted tool calls (e.g., the right function name and
  parameters).]{.c5}

[What is the Human-in-the-Loop (HITL) pattern?]{.c4} [ HITL is a pattern
that integrates human oversight and interaction into the agent\'s
workflow. Instead of being fully autonomous, the agent pauses at
critical junctures to ask for human feedback, approval, clarification,
or direction.]{.c5}

[Why is HITL important for agentic systems?]{.c4} [ It\'s crucial for
several reasons:]{.c5}

- [Safety and Control:]{.c4} [ For high-stakes tasks (e.g., financial
  transactions, sending official communications), HITL ensures a human
  verifies the agent\'s proposed actions before they are executed.]{.c5}
- [Improving Quality:]{.c4} [ Humans can provide corrections or nuanced
  feedback that the agent can use to improve its performance, especially
  in subjective or ambiguous tasks.]{.c5}
- [Building Trust:]{.c4} [ Users are more likely to trust and adopt an
  AI system that they can guide and supervise.]{.c5}

[At what points in a workflow should you include a human?]{.c4} [ Common
points for human intervention include:]{.c5}

- [Plan Approval:]{.c4} [ Before executing a multi-step plan.]{.c5}
- [Tool Use Confirmation:]{.c4} [ Before using a tool that has
  real-world consequences or costs money.]{.c5}
- [Ambiguity Resolution:]{.c4} [ When the agent is unsure how to proceed
  or needs more information from the user.]{.c5}
- [Final Output Review:]{.c4} [ Before delivering the final result to
  the end-user or system.]{.c5}

[Isn\'t constant human intervention inefficient?]{.c4} [ It can be,
which is why the key is to find the right balance. HITL should be
implemented at critical checkpoints, not for every single action. The
goal is to build a collaborative partnership between the human and the
agent, where the agent handles the bulk of the work and the human
provides strategic guidance.]{.c5}

[What is the Multi-Agent Collaboration pattern?]{.c4} [ This pattern
involves creating a system composed of multiple specialized agents that
work together to achieve a common goal. Instead of one \"generalist\"
agent trying to do everything, you create a team of \"specialist\"
agents, each with a specific role or expertise.]{.c5}

[What are the benefits of a multi-agent system?]{.c8}

- [Modularity and Specialization:]{.c4} [ Each agent can be fine-tuned
  and prompted for its specific task (e.g., a \"researcher\" agent, a
  \"writer\" agent, a \"code\" agent), leading to higher quality
  results.]{.c5}
- [Reduced Complexity:]{.c4} [ Breaking a complex workflow down into
  specialized roles makes the overall system easier to design, debug,
  and maintain.]{.c5}
- [Simulated Brainstorming:]{.c4} [ Different agents can offer different
  perspectives on a problem, leading to more creative and robust
  solutions, similar to how a human team works.]{.c5}

[What is a common architecture for multi-agent systems?]{.c4} [ A common
architecture involves an ]{.calibre} [Orchestrator Agent]{.c4}
[ (sometimes called a \"manager\" or \"conductor\"). The orchestrator
understands the overall goal, breaks it down, and delegates sub-tasks to
the appropriate specialist agents. It then collects the results from the
specialists and synthesizes them into a final output.]{.c5}

[How do the agents communicate with each other?]{.c4} [ Communication is
often managed by the orchestrator. For example, the orchestrator might
pass the output of the \"researcher\" agent to the \"writer\" agent as
context. A shared \"scratchpad\" or message bus where agents can post
their findings is another common communication method.]{.c5}

[Why is evaluating an agent more difficult than evaluating a traditional
software program?]{.c4} [ Traditional software has deterministic outputs
(the same input always produces the same output). Agents, especially
those using LLMs, are non-deterministic and their performance can be
subjective. Evaluating them requires assessing the ]{.calibre}
[quality]{.c9} [ and ]{.calibre} [relevance]{.c9} [ of their output, not
just whether it\'s technically \"correct.\"]{.c5}

[What are some common methods for evaluating agent performance?]{.c4}
[ The guide suggests a few methods:]{.c5}

- [Outcome-based Evaluation:]{.c4} [ Did the agent successfully achieve
  the final goal? For example, if the task was \"book a flight,\" was a
  flight actually booked correctly? This is the most important
  measure.]{.c5}
- [Process-based Evaluation:]{.c4} [ Was the agent\'s ]{.calibre}
  [process]{.c9} [ efficient and logical? Did it use the right tools?
  Did it follow a sensible plan? This helps debug why an agent might be
  failing.]{.c5}
- [Human Evaluation:]{.c4} [ Having humans score the agent\'s
  performance on a scale (e.g., 1-5) based on criteria like helpfulness,
  accuracy, and coherence. This is crucial for user-facing
  applications.]{.c5}

[What is an \"agent trajectory\"?]{.c4} [ An agent trajectory is the
complete log of an agent\'s steps while performing a task. It includes
all its thoughts, actions (tool calls), and observations. Analyzing
these trajectories is a key part of debugging and understanding agent
behavior.]{.c5}

[How can you create reliable tests for a non-deterministic system?]{.c4}
[ While you can\'t guarantee the exact wording of an agent\'s output,
you can create tests that check for key elements. For example, you can
write a test that verifies if the agent\'s final response ]{.calibre}
[contains]{.c9} [ specific information or if it successfully called a
certain tool with the right parameters. This is often done using mock
tools in a dedicated testing environment.]{.c5}

[How is prompting an agent different from a simple ChatGPT prompt?]{.c4}
[ Prompting an agent involves creating a detailed \"system prompt\" or
constitution that acts as its operating instructions. This goes beyond a
single user query; it defines the agent\'s role, its available tools,
the patterns it should follow (like ReAct or Planning), its constraints,
and its personality.]{.c5}

[What are the key components of a good system prompt for an agent?]{.c4}
[ A strong system prompt typically includes:]{.c5}

- [Role and Goal:]{.c4} [ Clearly define who the agent is and what its
  primary purpose is.]{.c5}
- [Tool Definitions:]{.c4} [ A list of available tools, their
  descriptions, and how to use them (e.g., in a specific
  function-calling format).]{.c5}
- [Constraints and Rules:]{.c4} [ Explicit instructions on what the
  agent ]{.calibre} [should not]{.c9} [ do (e.g., \"Do not use tools
  without approval,\" \"Do not provide financial advice\").]{.c5}
- [Process Instructions:]{.c4} [ Guidance on which patterns to use. For
  example, \"First, create a plan. Then, execute the plan
  step-by-step.\"]{.c5}
- [Example Trajectories:]{.c4} [ Providing a few examples of successful
  \"thought-action-observation\" loops can significantly improve the
  agent\'s reliability.]{.c5}

[What is \"prompt leakage\"?]{.c4} [ Prompt leakage occurs when parts of
the system prompt (like tool definitions or internal instructions) are
inadvertently revealed in the agent\'s final response to the user. This
can be confusing for the user and expose underlying implementation
details. Techniques like using separate prompts for reasoning and for
generating the final answer can help prevent this.]{.c5}

[What are some future trends in agentic systems?]{.c4} [ The guide
points towards a future with:]{.c5}

- [More Autonomous Agents:]{.c4} [ Agents that require less human
  intervention and can learn and adapt on their own.]{.c5}
- [Highly Specialized Agents:]{.c4} [ An ecosystem of agents that can be
  hired or subscribed to for specific tasks (e.g., a travel agent, a
  research agent).]{.c5}
- [Better Tools and Platforms:]{.c4} [ The development of more
  sophisticated frameworks and platforms that make it easier to build,
  test, and deploy robust multi-agent systems.]{.c5}

[]{.c5}
